body.question_1: How would you rate the technical competency of the student intern in their relevant field of study?
body.answer_1: The intern from the School of Engineering's Electrical Engineering program showed adequate technical competency, with a solid theoretical foundation that required additional scaffolding to translate effectively into professional practice. Core technical knowledge in the primary discipline areas was present and generally accurate, enabling the intern to engage meaningfully with assigned tasks after an initial onboarding period. However, familiarity with specific industry-standard tools and processes was limited, requiring time investment from senior staff to bridge the gap. Stronger emphasis on applied tool training and industry-facing projects within the curriculum would produce more immediately effective graduates and reduce the onboarding burden on host companies.

body.question_2: How well did the intern demonstrate professional communication and interpersonal skills in the workplace?
body.answer_2: The professional attitude demonstrated by this intern from the School of Engineering's Electrical Engineering program was excellent and genuinely impressed our team throughout the internship period. Punctuality, reliability, and a consistently positive and collaborative work ethic were evident from the very first week, requiring no correction or management intervention to maintain. The intern communicated proactively, raised concerns and questions through appropriate channels, and responded to feedback constructively and without defensiveness. Their professional maturity — particularly in managing competing deadlines, handling ambiguity, and engaging respectfully with colleagues at all levels — reflects a level of preparation that speaks well of the School of Engineering's commitment to producing work-ready graduates.

body.question_3: How effectively did the intern collaborate with team members and contribute to group projects?
body.answer_3: This intern's teamwork skills were fair, with a genuine willingness to collaborate balanced by some developmental areas that became apparent as project complexity increased. The intern worked well in low-ambiguity team tasks with clear role definitions, but required additional support when navigating more complex team dynamics involving competing priorities, unclear ownership, or conflicting perspectives. Communication within the team improved noticeably over the internship duration, which is an encouraging sign of learning and adaptability. Further emphasis on collaborative project work, cross-functional team exercises, and conflict resolution skills in the Electrical Engineering curriculum would produce more team-ready graduates for the professional environment.

body.question_4: How would you assess the intern's problem-solving abilities and approach to challenging or unfamiliar tasks?
body.answer_4: Problem-solving capabilities for this intern from the School of Engineering's Electrical Engineering program were decent, reflecting a sound foundational analytical approach with room for development in handling more complex, ambiguous challenges. For well-defined problems with clear parameters, the intern performed competently and demonstrated sound logical reasoning and structured analysis. However, when problems were ambiguous, multi-layered, or required creative reframing of assumptions, the intern benefited significantly from supervisor guidance to stay productive. Incorporating more open-ended, ill-defined problem scenarios into coursework and assessments would better prepare graduates for the messy, constraint-rich challenges of professional practice.

body.question_5: How quickly and effectively did the intern adapt to your company's work culture, tools, and environment?
body.answer_5: This intern showed satisfactory adaptability to our work environment, adjusting reasonably well to most situations while requiring additional support when changes occurred at a faster pace or scale than anticipated. Day-to-day routine adjustments — such as modified task priorities or new tool adoption — were handled efficiently and without notable disruption. More significant changes, such as project pivots or team restructuring, required more guidance to navigate smoothly and confidently. Greater exposure to agile methodologies, sprint-based workflows, and change management principles in the Electrical Engineering curriculum would strengthen the adaptability of graduates entering dynamic professional environments.

body.question_6: How proficient was the intern in English communication, both written and verbal, for professional purposes?
body.answer_6: English communication proficiency was a substandard limitation for this intern from the School of Engineering's Electrical Engineering program that impacted their effectiveness in several key areas of their role. Written communication — including emails and technical documentation — frequently contained grammatical and structural errors that required editing by supervisors before use in internal or external contexts. Verbal participation in English-language meetings was hesitant, limiting the intern's ability to contribute fully to discussions or to represent the team confidently in broader organizational settings. We strongly recommend the School of Engineering elevate its English communication training requirements — particularly for professional writing and spoken business English — as a core graduation competency for all programs.

body.question_7: How well did the intern's academic training align with the practical demands and standards of your industry?
body.answer_7: Based on our experience with this intern from the School of Engineering's Electrical Engineering program, we are impressively impressed with the alignment between the academic curriculum and the practical demands of our industry. The foundational knowledge, theoretical frameworks, and applied skills the intern brought to the role mapped closely to what we require in graduate-level professional practitioners in this field. In particular, the project-based and problem-solving components of the curriculum appear to have translated well into workplace readiness. We would encourage the School of Engineering to continue developing its industry connections and to involve practitioners actively in curriculum review to maintain this strong alignment as the professional landscape continues to evolve.

body.question_8: What specific technical or soft skill areas do you believe the university curriculum should strengthen for future graduates?
body.answer_8: Our experience with this intern has highlighted insufficient gaps between what the School of Engineering's Electrical Engineering curriculum delivers and what the professional industry actually requires from graduates in this field. Specifically, the intern lacked working proficiency in several tools and methodologies that are now industry standard, requiring us to invest significant onboarding time in areas that should be covered academically. Soft skills — particularly professional communication, project management, and stakeholder management — also appeared underdeveloped relative to what professional roles at this level require from day one. We would welcome the opportunity to engage with the School of Engineering's curriculum design team to provide specific, actionable recommendations for improvement.

body.question_9: How would you rate the intern's overall performance and value contribution to your organization during the placement?
body.answer_9: The overall performance of this intern from the School of Engineering's Electrical Engineering program has been excellent and has added genuine value to our organization during the internship period in ways we did not fully anticipate at the outset. Key deliverables were completed on time, to the required quality standard, and in some cases with additional initiative that exceeded the brief. The intern's contributions to ongoing team projects have been meaningful and will have a lasting impact beyond the duration of their placement. We are very satisfied with the investment we made in hosting this intern and have recommended internally that we continue and expand our partnership with the School of Engineering's internship program in the coming academic year.

body.question_10: Based on your experience with this intern, would you consider hiring from this university in the future, and why?
body.answer_10: While we respect the School of Engineering as an institution and do not question its academic credentials, our experience with this particular intern from the Electrical Engineering program has given us substandard reservations about recruiting heavily from this cohort in the near future. The skill gaps and professional readiness challenges encountered during this placement represent a significant onboarding investment that strains our capacity as a host organization. We would encourage the School of Engineering to engage directly with industry partners — including ourselves — to understand the specific competency gaps and develop targeted solutions before we could confidently increase our intake of graduates from this program. We remain open to future engagement if meaningful curriculum improvements are implemented and communicated to the industry community.
